papers.title: STEM Agent: A Self-Adapting, Tool-Enabled, Extensible Architecture for Multi-Protocol AI Agent Systems
papers.innovation: Introduces STEM Agent, an architecture designed to overcome limitations of current AI agent frameworks by offering a self-adapting, tool-enabled, extensible, and multi-protocol approach. This allows for dynamic adaptation to interaction paradigms, tool integration strategies, and user models, unlike rigid existing systems.
papers.benchmarks: Improved adaptability, extensibility, and deployment versatility across diverse interaction paradigms compared to existing rigid agent frameworks that commit early to single protocols, fixed tools, and static user models.
papers.use_case: Developing highly flexible, robust, and widely deployable AI agent systems that can operate across various interaction protocols, seamlessly integrate diverse tools, and adapt dynamically to user needs in complex, multi-agent environments.

papers.title: From Static Templates to Dynamic Runtime Graphs: A Survey of Workflow Optimization for LLM Agents
papers.innovation: A comprehensive survey that synthesizes and categorizes methods for designing and optimizing executable workflows for LLM-based systems. It highlights the evolution from static templates to dynamic runtime graphs, covering crucial components like LLM calls, information retrieval, tool use, code execution, memory updates, and verification.
papers.benchmarks: Reviews methods that improve performance in various LLM agent tasks such as complex reasoning, planning, and task execution by optimizing workflow design, reliability, and efficiency.
papers.use_case: Guiding AI engineers in designing, developing, and optimizing robust and efficient workflows for complex LLM-based agents, enabling better performance, reliability, and maintainability in applications requiring sophisticated multi-step reasoning and interaction.

papers.title: Session Risk Memory (SRM): Temporal Authorization for Deterministic Pre-Execution Safety Gates
papers.innovation: Introduces Session Risk Memory (SRM), a temporal authorization system designed to enhance AI agent safety. Unlike per-action authorization, SRM considers the accumulated context and historical actions of an agent over time, enabling detection of distributed attacks that decompose harmful intent across multiple individually compliant steps.
papers.benchmarks: Enhanced detection of distributed attacks and improved robustness against multi-step harmful agent behaviors, providing a more comprehensive safety net than traditional per-action authorization systems.
papers.use_case: Implementing advanced safety and security mechanisms for AI agents, particularly in sensitive or critical applications where agents perform sequences of actions. It helps in building more secure and trustworthy autonomous systems by preventing complex, multi-step harmful actions.
